prompt: A hand-drawn naturalist scientific illustration of a Glasswing Butterfly (Greta oto). a brush-footed butterfly (Greta oto) with wings spread, the defining feature being wing membranes that are GENUINELY TRANSPARENT like clear colorless glass — the cell panels between the veins are see-through so the warm cream background shows straight through them (NOT filled with opaque white, cream, or beige paint), crossed only by fine dark veins; each wing rimmed by an opaque reddish-brown to chocolate border edged with a thin faint orange line, plus a similar reddish-brown bar across the middle of each forewing; slender dark blackish body and thin dark antennae; the empty glass-clear panes are unmistakable. Vintage entomology field guide aesthetic with subtle ink and watercolor texture, warm earthy palette, single bug centered as hero on a plain warm cream/beige background (no scene, no environment), soft even lighting, gentle drop shadow beneath the bug, square 1:1 composition. CIRCULAR SAFE ZONE: the final image is cropped to a CIRCLE, so the ENTIRE insect — including every leg, antenna, wing, and tail streamer, plus its drop shadow — must sit fully inside the central inscribed circle with even breathing room on all sides. The bug occupies roughly 60-68 percent of the frame, comfortably centered; nothing touches or extends toward the edges or the four corners. Long appendages (antennae, legs, moth tails) are posed inward/curved so they stay within the circle rather than reaching the border. illustrated style not photorealistic, designed to be displayed as a circular thumbnail. ABSOLUTELY NO TEXT, NO LABELS, NO CAPTIONS, NO SPECIES NAMES, NO TAXONOMIC ANNOTATIONS, NO LATIN BINOMIAL, NO TYPOGRAPHY OF ANY KIND ANYWHERE IN THE IMAGE, NO SCALE BAR. The cream/beige background must be a SQUARE solid fill that completely covers the entire image edge to edge — NOT a circular or oval parchment vignette. The background fills all four corners. Pure illustration only, no field-guide caption block, no specimen-card framing, no decorative border, no parchment cutout shape.

A hand-drawn naturalist scientific illustration of a Lantern Bug (Pyrops candelaria). a tropical planthopper (Pyrops candelaria) shown in side profile, its single most distinctive feature a long slender hollow snout (cephalic process) projecting forward from the head and curving gently UPWARD at the tip like a narrow upturned horn or small elephant trunk, the snout bright reddish-orange dusted with tiny white speckles; FOREWINGS held roof-like along the back colored leaf-green with scattered small white and pale-yellow spots; the HINDWINGS beneath flashing bright golden-yellow bordered with black; body green flecked with white; six short legs; pose the upturned red snout and legs curved inward so everything stays inside the circle. A hand-drawn naturalist scientific illustration of a Saddleback Caterpillar (Acharia stimulea). a small slug-like stinging caterpillar (Acharia stimulea) viewed from the side, its single distinctive feature a vivid bright-green 'saddle-blanket' draped over the middle of its back bearing a large central oval purplish-brown 'saddle' spot ringed by a clean outline of WHITE then BLACK; the two stocky ends of the body (head and rear) are velvety purplish-brown, each end crowned with a pair of thick fleshy horn-like tubercles bristling with stiff stinging spines (four big spiny horn-clusters total), plus a fringe of smaller spiny tubercles along the lower sides; no obvious legs (sluglike), compact loaf-shaped body posed centered with even breathing room so the whole caterpillar and every spine sit well inside the circle.
